children.title: You said should i be concerned by these things in my w2?

children.value: should i be concerned by these things in my w2?

the short answer is no, you should not be concerned, provided the data on your physical paper w-2 matches exactly what you typed into the tax software. these are standard "sanity check" warnings designed to catch typos, not necessarily errors in the tax documents themselves.

this warning is appearing because your personal mailing address is in massachusetts (ma), but the wages were earned and taxed in california (ca). it is very common for people to move between states or work for a company based in a different state than their residence. as long as you did indeed work or earn that income in california, this is correct. the software is just flagging it because it is a common point of error for people who live and work in the same place.
this occurs because you entered an amount for state tax withheld in box 17, but the software noticed the corresponding employer state id field in box 15 is blank or was skipped.
if your physical w-2 has a number in box 15:
you should go back and enter it. this number is used by the state to track the payment.
if your physical w-2 is actually blank in box 15:
you can click continue. some states or specific employers use the federal ein (employer identification number) instead of a separate state id.
double check your physical w-2. if the software reflects exactly what is printed on that paper, you are fine to proceed.

children.title: You said is no state id number bad?

children.value: is no state id number bad?
it is not inherently bad, but it can occasionally cause a processing delay with the state’s department of revenue if they cannot immediately link the withholding to your employer.

in many cases, the state id is simply the same as the federal employer identification number (ein). if your physical w-2 has a blank box 15 but shows a dollar amount in box 17 (state income tax), the tax software might flag it as a "critical" warning, but it is not a legal dealbreaker.
some states allow employers to use their federal ein for state reporting purposes.
some states do not require a specific state id number for certain types of businesses or non-profits.
the payroll provider simply missed it when printing the form.
look for a number often formatted like
. even if it is not labeled "state id," any number in that box should be entered.
if box 15 is truly empty, look at box b (employer identification number). some people try entering that number into the state id field to see if the software accepts it.
if you leave it blank and the software allows you to e-file, the state will typically use the other employer information (name, address, and federal ein) to verify the taxes paid on your behalf.
if the state rejects the return, they will send a letter asking for clarification, but it is a common issue that is usually resolved without much trouble.
did you notice if your federal ein is listed anywhere else on the form?
Gemini is AI and can make mistakes.
